name: Electromagnetic Induction
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. Magnetic Flux
sections.body:
Magnetic flux through a surface is the surface integral of $\vec{B}$: $$\Phi_B = \int \vec{B}\cdot d\vec{A}$$ For a uniform field through a flat area: $\Phi_B = BA\cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{B}$ and the normal $\hat{n}$. Unit: weber (Wb) = T·m².

Flux is a scalar (with sign depending on orientation). Maximum when $\vec{B}\parallel\hat{n}$, zero when $\vec{B}\perp\hat{n}$.

sections.heading: 2. Faraday's Laws
sections.body:
Faraday's experiments showed that a changing magnetic flux through a circuit induces an EMF:

  • First law: an EMF is induced whenever the flux through a circuit changes.
  • Second law: the magnitude of induced EMF is proportional to the rate of change of flux: $$\varepsilon = -\dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$$

For a coil of $N$ turns: $\varepsilon = -N\,d\Phi_B/dt$. The induced current $I = \varepsilon/R$. Charge that flows in time $\Delta t$: $q = \Delta\Phi_B/R$ — independent of how fast the change happens.

sections.heading: 3. Lenz's Law and Conservation of Energy
sections.body:
Lenz's law: the direction of induced current is such that it opposes the change in flux that produced it.

This is a consequence of energy conservation. If the induced current aided the change, flux would grow without bound, providing free energy. Instead, the induced current creates a magnetic field opposing the original flux change, and external work must be done to maintain the change — that work appears as electrical (and thermal) energy in the circuit.

Examples:

  • Pushing N pole toward a coil → induced current makes the near face act like N (repels the magnet).
  • Pulling magnet away → induced current reverses, attracting magnet.

The negative sign in Faraday's law $\varepsilon = -d\Phi/dt$ encodes Lenz's law.

sections.heading: 4. Motional EMF
sections.body:
A conducting rod of length $l$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a uniform field $\vec{B}$ has free electrons pushed by the Lorentz force, accumulating charge until the resulting electric field balances the magnetic force. The induced (motional) EMF is $$\varepsilon = Bvl$$ Polarity: positive end is where positive charges accumulate (use $\vec{F} = q\vec{v}\times\vec{B}$).

If the rod is part of a closed circuit of resistance $R$, induced current $I = Bvl/R$, and force needed to keep moving: $F_{\text{ext}} = BIl = B^2 l^2 v/R$. Power delivered: $P = F_{\text{ext}} v = B^2 l^2 v^2/R$, which equals power dissipated $I^2 R$.

sections.heading: 5. Eddy Currents
sections.body:
When a bulk conductor experiences a changing magnetic flux, induced currents flow in closed loops within it — eddy currents. They oppose the change (Lenz) and dissipate energy as heat.

Applications: induction heating (e.g. cooktops, heat treatment of metals), electromagnetic braking (in trains and electric vehicles), speedometers, dead-beat galvanometers (rapid damping).

Drawbacks: cause power loss in transformer and motor cores. Mitigated by laminating the core (thin sheets separated by insulation) to break up the loops.

sections.heading: 6. Self-Inductance
sections.body:
A current $I$ in a coil produces flux through the coil itself, proportional to $I$: $\Phi = LI$. The proportionality constant $L$ is the self-inductance. Unit: henry (H) = V·s/A = Wb/A.

Changing current induces a back-EMF: $$\varepsilon = -L\dfrac{dI}{dt}$$

For a long solenoid (length $l$, area $A$, $n$ turns/length, total $N = nl$): $$L = \mu_0 n^2 A l = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}$$

Energy stored in inductor: $U = \dfrac{1}{2}LI^2$. Energy density in field: $u = B^2/(2\mu_0)$.

sections.heading: 7. Mutual Inductance
sections.body:
Two coupled coils: changing current $I_1$ in coil 1 produces flux $\Phi_2$ in coil 2 proportional to $I_1$: $\Phi_2 = M I_1$, where $M$ is the mutual inductance. Induced EMF in coil 2: $\varepsilon_2 = -M\,dI_1/dt$. By symmetry $M_{12} = M_{21}$.

For two coaxial solenoids (inner $n_1$ turns/length, outer $n_2$, common area $A$, length $l$): $$M = \mu_0 n_1 n_2 A l$$

Coupling coefficient: $k = M/\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$, with $0 \le k \le 1$. $k = 1$ for ideal coupling (used in transformers).

sections.heading: 8. AC Generator
sections.body:
Mechanical energy is converted to electrical energy by rotating a coil in a magnetic field. A coil of $N$ turns, area $A$, rotating at angular frequency $\omega$ in field $\vec{B}$ generates flux $\Phi = NBA\cos(\omega t)$.

Induced EMF: $$\varepsilon = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = NBA\omega\sin(\omega t) = \varepsilon_0\sin(\omega t)$$ where peak EMF $\varepsilon_0 = NBA\omega$. Output is sinusoidal AC.

Components: armature (rotating coil), field magnet, slip rings (for AC) or commutator (for DC), brushes. Frequency in India: 50 Hz.

summary: Faraday: $\varepsilon = -d\Phi_B/dt$. Lenz: induced current opposes the flux change (energy conservation). Motional EMF $\varepsilon = Bvl$. Eddy currents heat conductors and damp motion. Self-inductance $\varepsilon = -L\,dI/dt$, energy $\tfrac{1}{2}LI^2$, density $B^2/(2\mu_0)$. Mutual inductance couples two coils, basis of transformer. AC generator gives $\varepsilon = NBA\omega\sin\omega t$.
pitfalls: Faraday's law gives EMF, not current — current depends on resistance too.
Lenz's law gives DIRECTION; sign is automatic via the minus sign in dΦ/dt.
Charge induced (q = ΔΦ/R) depends only on net flux change, NOT on rate.
An inductor opposes change in current, not current itself — at steady DC it acts as a wire.
Energy in inductor lives in the magnetic field (u = B²/2μ₀), analogous to capacitor's E²/2 ε₀.
Eddy currents are mitigated by lamination — break up the conducting paths.
